data.title: Tesla Wants To Become Nvidia - It Can't
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has announced its most surprising and unrealistic pivot yet: entering the AI semiconductor race.

CEO Elon Musk claims Tesla will design and manufacture a new AI chip every 12 months, eventually producing “more AI chips than all competitors combined.”

This comes just weeks after Tesla approved a trillion-dollar CEO compensation package based on already optimistic milestones.

20 million vehicle sales. 10 million full self-drive ("FSD") subscribers, a million robotaxis, a million humanoid robots, and hundreds of billions in EBITDA.

Now, Tesla is adding yet another trillion-dollar industry to its to-do list.

I’m afraid Tesla, under the guidance of Elon Musk, is jumping at every new shiny AI trend, and this won’t end well.

And as an investor, this is where I completely lose patience.

Tesla’s New AI Chip Plan: A Promise Detached From Reality

Tesla’s latest announcement is nothing short of staggering. Musk stated publicly:

Our goal is to bring a new AI chip design to volume production every 12 months… We expect to build chips at higher volumes ultimately than all other AI chips combined.

Source: Elon Musk.

Nvidia (NVDA), the undisputed leader in AI compute (for now), operates on development cycles closer to 18–24 months.

These cycles are supported by tens of thousands of engineers, billions in annual R&D spending, multi-decade compiler development, deeply embedded software stacks, and partnerships with TSMC (TSM) that took more than a decade to build.

Tesla has no such infrastructure, but somehow Musk is suggesting Tesla can out-engineer the entire semiconductor industry in a single jump.

This is a statement detached from operational reality.

Designing AI Chips Is Nothing Like Designing FSD Hardware

A core misconception behind Tesla’s new ambition is the belief that designing chipsets for Full Self-Driving is somehow a stepping stone to building world-class AI training silicon. The two could not be further apart.

FSD hardware, even in its most advanced form, is fundamentally a custom SoC powering inference models on consumer devices.

It is complex, yes, but nowhere near the architectural depth, memory coherence, bandwidth, and compiler ecosystem required to design chips that compete with Nvidia’s H100, Blackwell, Google’s TPUs, or Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) MI300 series.

AI training accelerators require:

  • Integrated compiler environments

  • Highly optimized networking fabrics

  • Multigenerational architectural roadmaps

  • Tight integration with cloud infrastructure

  • And, most importantly, massive internal workloads to test and improve the chip.

Tesla has none of these.

The company has no CUDA-like ecosystem, no large language model research lab, no enterprise customer base, no cloud infrastructure and no production-scale AI training workloads outside of FSD, which remains pre-commercial.

Trying to jump from commodity inference SoCs to high-end AI training silicon is like trying to leap from designing smartphone chips to designing a supercomputer. There is simply no bridge.

The Economics Are Even More Absurd

Even if the technical challenge weren’t insurmountable, the financial requirements would be.

Nvidia spends roughly $30 billion per year between R&D and capital expenditure to maintain its lead.

Google has invested more than a decade building its TPU ecosystem. Meta (META) is already on its second generation of MTIA silicon solely for internal workloads.

AMD has spent more than twenty years building its GPU and ROCm ecosystem.

And most of these companies have strong cash flows, multi-year engineering benches, and stable demand to amortize development costs.

Meanwhile, TSLA’s margins have fallen for two years straight.

Capex is already stretched thin across new gigafactories, battery production, the robotaxi program, and Optimus.

And the company generates no high-margin cloud revenue, has no enterprise AI customers, and remains deeply unprofitable in autonomy.

A Strategy At War With Itself

This new chip initiative directly contradicts what Tesla should be doing.

In my previous article, I argued that Tesla must choose between being:

  • A high-volume EV manufacturer with improving margins, or

  • An AI/robotics platform betting on autonomy.

Both paths require intense focus and enormous resources. Instead, Tesla is now adding a third ambition: becoming a semiconductor powerhouse.

Investors like myself were looking for clarity, but instead, they’ve been handed yet another pivot into an industry where Tesla has no competitive footing.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, every Musk statement as of late seems to be a distraction from the harsh reality that Tesla isn’t actually doing that well.

The company is facing slowing EV demand, thin margins, increased competition from BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY) and years of overpromising on autonomy.

Rather than focusing on what once made the company great, Tesla keeps trying to redefine itself, and this is an idea I can’t get behind anymore, without evidence that it’s working.

There is no evidence Tesla has the technical talent, financial resources, or organizational discipline to accomplish any of these goals, let alone all of them simultaneously.

And while Musk’s dream keeps expanding, Tesla’s execution simply cannot match this ambition.

A an investor, Tesla’s latest pivot only reinforces my view:

Tesla is out of its depth in AI, lacks strategic focus, and is no longer a company I can be bullish on.

data.title: Ferrari: The Path To Regain A 40x Multiple
data.description:
Ferrari (RACE) has seen its shares plummet nearly 25% since its disappointing October Investor Day, where the company set unambitious guidance for the rest of the decade.

The company's valuation multiple, which hovered near the 50s not so long ago, compressed sharply, with the stock now trading in the low 30s based on next year's earnings estimates.

After the company laid out a future of mid-single-digit EPS growth, relying on earnings growth alone won't suffice for market-beating returns. They will have to fuel a multiple expansion story as well.

So, let's detail Ferrari's path to market-beating returns and assess whether it's time to get back on the prancing horse.

The Shadow Over Ferrari's Stock

Ever since Ferrari went public in 2015, it has mostly been up and to the right. In the span of 10 years, the stock became a nine-bagger, as revenue more than doubled and earnings grew nearly 5x.

Throughout this period, Ferrari has built a reputation as a company that underpromises and overdelivers, as they consistently came way above their guidance targets, including surpassing their 2026 estimates more than a year earlier.

This reflected both a humble and conservative approach from management and also upside surprises, especially when it comes to customer willingness to pay much higher prices, as well as spend heavily on car personalizations.

It got to a point where investors almost ignored the company's guidance, and analysts have set their own much higher targets. But after a weak Q2, management's very low guidance for 2030 caught investors at a moment of lower confidence.

Now, the story is simple. As long as investors take Ferrari's guidance as is, the stock is unlikely to deliver strong returns. Simply put, the only way the stock returns to its glory days is if investors will once again set their own targets and ignore the 5% target that's overshadowing Ferrari's shares.

Therefore, the question that Ferrari shareholders should ask is: What's the path to remove that shadow?

The Path To Regain A Premium Multiple Goes Through Execution

I wish I had some very complicated or creative thing to say here, but in reality, this story is quite clear to those who have been following Ferrari for some time.

The path to market-beating returns from here goes through consistent beats from Ferrari. In other words, this has now become a "show-me" story, where investors will need to see several consecutive quarters of higher-than-5% growth before they can go back to underwriting it as a long-term trajectory.

Looking at consensus estimates from 2026 onwards, analysts estimate Ferrari's revenue growth will gradually decelerate to the 5% pace the company guided for. This would mean a growth pace that's literally 50% lower than what investors grew accustomed to.

Of course, with lower growth expectations comes a lower valuation, as Ferrari is far removed from the 50x P/E club it once belonged to. That said, as noted in the graph above, Ferrari is still not at a trough valuation either.

So, can Ferrari deliver outsized growth in the near term and reignite investor confidence?

Q3 Was A Step In The Right Direction, But A Small One

Ferrari revenues in Q3 were €1.8 billion, up 7% Y/Y and 9% on a constant currency basis. This marks an important acceleration from the 4.4% in Q2, and reflects a nice beat over estimates.

Shipments were up 0.5%, while the average selling price rose 5%. Meanwhile, Sponsorship & brand revenues continue to lead the way, growing 21% Y/Y. Operating margin expanded slightly, while EBITDA margin dropped by 90 bps.

For Ferrari to generate steady high-single-digit revenue growth, it needs to perfectly time the launch of new models with the end-of-life of previous ones, to create an organic rise in average selling price without undertaking same-model price increases. On that last point, it's key to understand that Ferrari does most of its selling upfront (cc: sold-out order books), which means it doesn't really have the opportunity to raise prices on the same model.

We are now entering a period where it seems that this timing wasn't perfect. In addition, it seems that the all-electric Ferrari will be selling at a below-average price, and that the company's overall enthusiasm for its long-term contribution has waned.

These concerns haven't been alleviated (and weren't going to be) in Q3, and likely won't be answered for quite some time. So, in the near term, investors will have to rely on valuation.

Valuation

Here's what investors can't do, in my mind. I don't believe investors can just assume that because it's Ferrari, it will eventually get back to its previous share price.

We've laid out the reasons why not: lower growth trajectory, concerns about ASP growth runway, and caution about the Elettrica.

At 34 times next year's estimates and 22 times EBITDA, I believe that Ferrari is priced like it won't answer these questions anytime soon, but on the other hand, it's priced like it's going to deliver high-single-digit growth, which is higher than their guidance pace.

I'm not so confident in their ability to deliver that, and even if they do, I don't think it will be enough to drive great returns.

To me, around 30 times earnings is where investors will get all the optionality for free, and have a low enough downside risk. Until then, I think the right thing to do is to remain on the sidelines.

Conclusion

Ferrari's shares are still pressured by the Investor Day disappointment, as questions about the company's ability to beat its 5% growth outlook remain.

In my mind, there's not much Ferrari can do to answer these questions in the near to mid-term, as we enter a period of major changes in the model portfolio.

Even after the 25% drop, I still think the stock doesn't have enough reward to justify the risk, and I'd rather put my money elsewhere.

As such, I reiterate Ferrari at a "Hold."

data.title: VTWO: A Close Look At Vanguard's Low-Cost Russell 2000 Index ETF
data.description:
Introduction

With a 0.07% expense ratio, the Vanguard Russell 2000 Index ETF (VTWO) is the cheapest Russell 2000 Index ETF on the market, providing investors with simple exposure to stocks ranked #1001-3000 by total market capitalization. For those unfamiliar, this article describes VTWO's strategy, performance, and fund characteristics in detail, though I hope that all readers will benefit from the in-depth fundamental analysis I'll provide that compares VTWO against other popular benchmarks and a few factor-based funds worth considering. Ultimately, my goal is that after reading this article, you'll have a good idea if VTWO is right for you, and that you'll feel more confident about investing in small caps in general. Please enjoy the read, and as always, I look forward to your questions in the comments section afterward.

Vanguard Russell 2000 Index ETF Overview

Strategy Discussion and Index Construction Process

VTWO tracks the Russell 2000 Index, one of the most popular benchmarks for U.S. small-cap company performance. The term "small cap" isn't very well-defined, but in this case, it's simply all the stocks ranked #1001-3000 by total market capitalization, as noted in the Russell US Equity Indexes methodology document. Still, there are a few nuances I'd like to point out before moving on with the analysis. Here are the key steps to creating the Index.

1. Russell Indexes capture approximately 99% of the U.S. equity market and 100% of the investable U.S. market. Each June, the Indexes are completely rebuilt, with the broadest being the Russell 3000E Index, which contains the 4,000 largest eligible U.S. companies by total market capitalization.

2. Eligible securities must have the U.S. as their country of incorporation, country of headquarters, and country of the most liquid exchange, as defined by two-year average daily dollar trading volume. The Index has tiebreaker rules in place to handle conflicts.

3. Eligible securities must trade on either the CBOE, NYSE, NYSE American, NASDAQ, or ARCA exchanges, have a closing price on its primary exchange at or above $1.00 per share, have a market capitalization above $30 million, and have more than 5% free float available for trading.

4. All eligible securities are grouped into an Index, though current Russell 1000 Index securities ranking outside the top 1000 may retain their membership if their market cap, as measured cumulatively, is within a 2.5% percentile range (see example below). Similarly, a current Russell 2000 Index member may not be moved to the Russell 1000 Index if their market capitalization does not pass the threshold.

5. After securities are assigned to an Index, they are weighted by their float-adjusted market capitalizations. The rank day is the last business day of April, while changes are implemented after the close of business on the fourth Friday in June.

Many investors prefer Russell Indexes because they're completely transparent, whereas membership of the S&P Composite 1500 Index, a division of which is the S&P SmallCap 600 Index, is more discretionary, given how it's managed by a Committee. The S&P Composite 1500 Index also has a profit requirement for new entrants, so it's possible it might miss out on some large gains of exciting new companies.

For example, Amazon (AMZN) skipped joining the S&P SmallCap 600 and instead joined the S&P 500 in November 2005 after clearing the committee's size and profitability requirements, among others. However, as noted below, it was added to the Russell 2000 Index in June 1997, and gained 978% in the subsequent twelve months before being moved to the Russell 1000.

VTWO Fund Facts and Characteristics

The following is a list of VTWO's fund facts and characteristics pulled primarily from VTWO's website, fact sheet, annual financial statements, and quote page on Seeking Alpha.

  • Ticker: VTWO
  • Exchange: Nasdaq
  • Fund Type: Small-Cap Blend
  • Strategy: Passive
  • Benchmark Index: Russell 2000 Index
  • Inception Date: September 20, 2010
  • Assets Under Management: $13.9B
  • Shares Outstanding: 136,650,030
  • 25-Day Average Daily Volume: 2,483,144
  • Expense Ratio: 0.07%
  • 30-Day Median Bid/Ask Spread: 0.01%
  • Premium / Discount: 0.06%
  • 30-Day SEC Yield: 1.16%
  • TTM Distribution Yield: 1.18%
  • Distribution Schedule: Quarterly
  • Portfolio Turnover Rate: 14% (12-23% from 2021-2024)
  • Top GICS Sectors: Industrials (18%), Health Care (17%), Financials (17%)
  • Top 10 Holdings: 5.79%
  • Current Equity Holdings: 1,989

VTWO's expense ratio was recently lowered from 0.10% to 0.07% as part of the largest expense ratio reduction in Vanguard's history. VTWO's fee now undercuts the Global X Russell 2000 ETF (RSSL) by one basis point, though the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) remains the largest by size despite its higher 0.19% expense ratio.

IWM had the first-mover advantage, having launched a decade earlier in May 2000, but it also has a huge options market, whereas VTWO's is essentially non-existent. Therefore, I suggest VTWO is the better product for long-only investors, while IWM is more appropriate for traders.

Vanguard Russell 2000 Index ETF Performance

Small Caps vs. Large Caps (VTWO vs. VONE)

As of November 30, 2025, small caps are on track to underperform large caps for the ninth straight year, which is unprecedented. As shown below, VTWO trailed the Vanguard Russell 1000 ETF (VONE) by 12.78% in 2024 and last outperformed in 2016 by 9.59%, so it's really been a tough decade or so for small-cap stocks.

In terms of downside risk, VTWO (backfilled with IWM) recovered much faster than VONE (backfilled with IWB) from its drawdown from September 2000 to September 2022 (13 months vs. 49 months). Small caps also recovered quicker during the Great Financial Crisis (24 months vs. 37 months), so there's perhaps a case to be made that they're better bets during extreme periods of market stress. Otherwise, the large caps seem to recover quicker.

VTWO Performance vs. Small-Cap Blend ETFs

As of November 30, 2025, VTWO has delivered a 140.88% ten-year total return, which works out to be 9.19% annualized. The result places VTWO in the bottom half of the small-cap blend category, though you can see from the table how there is a large cluster of funds with returns in the 135-150% range, including the Vanguard S&P Small-Cap 600 ETF (VIOO). Notably, several higher fee factor funds have performed better, though I think it's safe to say that VTWO and VIOO are pretty reliable choices for getting "market-like" returns in the small-cap segment.

This table also includes each ETF's current weighted average market cap, and, to an earlier point, it demonstrates how the "small-cap" segment isn't very well defined. For example, the Vanguard Small Cap ETF (VB) sounds like a logical comparator, but its market cap is more than twice the size of VTWO's ($11.5B vs. $4.7B), so it's clearly pulling from the mid-cap segment. Among the 64 small-cap blend ETFs I track, the median market cap is VTWO's $4.6B, so it's right around what I think a small-cap ETF should represent.

When we expand the analysis to include all small-cap ETFs with at least a five-year track record, VTWO's 47.11% total return ranks only #34/40. VIOO did slightly better, gaining 53.63%, but again, several higher fee factor ETFs were the best performers. Several are also well-diversified, based on the percentage of assets allocated to their top ten industries, so I think it's worth looking at a few of them today to see what strengths they might have over VTWO.

Vanguard Russell 2000 Index ETF Analysis

VTWO Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

As of November 30, 2025, VTWO's top two sectors are Industrials (18%) and Health Care (17%), while its smallest sectors are Consumer Staples (3%) and Communication Services (2%). However, its sector allocations appear pretty standard for the small-cap blend ETF category. Based on the table below, VTWO overweights Health Care and underweights Consumer Discretionary by 3% and 2% compared to its peer group, but otherwise, this is what you can generally expect from a broad-based small-cap fund.

I chose these peers because they all have market caps between $4.5 and $6.0 billion, they all have outperformed VTWO over the last five years, and they are all well-diversified, with less than 40% allocated to their top ten industries. As such, I'm hoping there is something obvious from a factor perspective that will clue us in to what really matters when investing in small caps, so let's take a look at that next.

VTWO Fundamental Analysis

The following table highlights selected fundamental metrics for VTWO's top 15 holdings, which comprise 7.87% of the portfolio. Unfortunately, due to this low concentration level, I don't think there's much point listing more than that, as it's the actual summary metrics at the bottom that matter most. Therefore, please have a quick scan of those and how VTWO compares with its peers, and I'll offer some takeaways afterward.

#1: VTWO's Quality Features Are Generally Low

One feature common to all the outperforming ETFs is higher quality, evidenced by statistics such as EBIT margins, net margins, and return on total capital. For example, on a weighted average basis, VTWO's net margins are only 5.07% compared to 9.65% for VIOO, which makes sense, given the latter's profitability requirement for new entrants. Several factor funds like JHSC, PSC, and, to a lesser extent, FESM, also appear to emphasize higher profitability, so the takeaway here is that VTWO might just hold too many junk stocks that are too speculative. Recall how the Index purposely doesn't screen those out, as its primary objective is to design an Index that accurately reflects the small-cap segment at each reconstitution.

#2: Missing Profitability Data Makes Valuing ETFs Challenging

When calculating valuation ratios for portfolios, it's standard to exclude stocks with negative P/E ratios from the calculation. Morningstar follows this approach, as do I, but we see from the above table how several stocks have missing data, which indicates they're not yet profitable. Therefore, VTWO's 16.27x forward P/E ratio requires some important context, such as:

  • Only 72% of the portfolio by weight is profitable
  • Many stocks have forward P/E's exceeding 60x, Morningstar's cap.
  • VTWO's forward P/E jumps to 20.44x when applying the 60x cap to the 28% of unprofitable stocks.

Dealing with outliers and missing data is a challenging task for analysts and data providers, but the takeaway I have for readers is that they need to be extra careful about drawing any conclusions about a small-cap ETF's P/E ratio. Chances are the analyst will not make it clear that 28% of the calculation is missing, or that a further 7.5% had their P/E's capped at 60x.

However, you can draw your own inferences based on margin levels, which often are listed by data providers, or by simply relying on other valuation ratios that likely do include nearly all of an ETF's components. For example, P/CF captures 79% of VTWO, while EV/Sales and P/S capture 84% and 96%, respectively. In my view, these ratios are much more relevant than P/E, and VTWO's figures look reasonably competitive with its peers.

#3: Growth Potential Is VTWO's Biggest Strength

VTWO's components have increased sales by 11.46% per year over the last three years, which is second best among its peer group. Analysts also expect 12.31% and 12.08% sales and earnings per share growth over the next twelve months, which is also above average. In my view, these numbers suggest the Russell 2000 has more growth potential than the S&P SmallCap 600, and I think that helps to explain why VTWO has beaten VIOO in recent years.

Notably, FESM and PSC have even higher estimated earnings growth rates. However, in general, it's easier to do that when you aren't "forced" to own the entire small-cap segment. That holds true for any factor, so investors should weigh the advantages of these funds' superior factor mix against weaker company-level diversification.

Summary and Conclusion

VTWO has a 0.07% expense ratio, $13.9B in assets under management, and may be appropriate for long-only investors seeking to own the entire U.S. small-cap market for a minimal cost. This analysis highlighted how the Russell 2000 Index has lagged the Russell 1000 Index in nine of the last ten years, though there is some evidence that it could perform better and recover quicker in extreme market downturns, such as the tech crash of the early 2000s and the Great Financial Crisis. VTWO has also delivered middle-of-the-road returns against its small-cap peers over the last decade, but it has struggled over the last five, with higher-quality factor funds outperforming significantly.

From a factor perspective, VTWO's strength is its high growth potential, which is the likely source of its strong recent returns relative to S&P SmallCap 600 Index ETFs like VIOO. Meanwhile, its primary weakness is below-average quality, which also complicates valuation analysis, since roughly 30% of its portfolio is not profitable. Still, its lack of profitability screen can sometimes be beneficial. If the next Amazon is somewhere around the corner, it could spend years in the Russell Index family before joining the committee-managed S&P 1500 Composite Index.

This article was written primarily for educational purposes and, as such, did not include a rating. Instead, my main objective was to answer these four main questions about VTWO:

  1. What type of investor is VTWO designed for?
  2. How has VTWO performed against Russell 1000 Index ETFs?
  3. How has VTWO performed against other small-cap peers?
  4. What are VTWO's strengths and weaknesses from a factor perspective?

I hope I have answered the above questions, but if anything is unclear or you are interested in learning more about the alternatives to VTWO, please let me know in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Solana Struggles As Flows Weaken And Derivatives Tilt Risky (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Solana (SOL-USD) is attempting to stabilize after one of its sharpest corrective phases of the year, yet the character of the bounce remains fragile. Price has rebounded into a heavy resistance zone, but the broader trend continues to reflect forced positioning and a market still dominated by defensive behavior.

The market is trying to form a base near $135–$145, but momentum remains weak and structural pressure persists across spot and derivatives markets.

Trend pressure remains dominant

Solana’s collapse from highs above $270 pushed the token through the 20-day, 50-day, 100-day, and 200-day EMAs, confirming the transition from expansion to liquidation. Every rebound attempt since October has been capped by a descending trendline now aligned with resistance at $155–$157. That zone overlaps with short-term moving averages and continues to define the market’s ceiling until decisively broken.

SOL price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Despite the recent bounce, the candles remain shallow with limited follow-through, consistent with short covering rather than new accumulation. The EMAs are still bearish-stacked and the Supertrend remains red, underscoring that Solana remains in a downtrend until proven otherwise. A decisive break and close above $157 is required to shift the tone from corrective bounce to structural recovery.

The $135 support zone is the immediate downside pivot. Losing that level exposes $120, where liquidity clusters historically sit. Until price clears higher ground, bears maintain the strategic advantage.

Flows signal weak demand

Spot flows reflect the same fragility. December has recorded persistent outflows, including nearly $16 million in the latest session. This pattern highlights fading organic demand and ongoing distribution from larger holders who are selling into rebounds. Outflows appear to be shrinking, which may signal that the forced selling phase is ending, but there is still no evidence of credible accumulation.

Derivatives positioning adds complexity. Open interest sits around $7.4 billion, an elevated level that leaves the market leveraged and vulnerable to volatility. OI is no longer expanding, suggesting shorts have stopped building but also that fresh conviction from longs remains limited. Liquidations over the past day hit shorts more heavily, helping fuel the bounce, but such squeezes rarely transition into sustained rallies without genuine spot demand.

Long/short ratios tell the clearest story. Binance prints ratios above 2.0, showing traders lean aggressively long into resistance. This imbalance is dangerous if the price fails at $157, as crowded long positioning can unwind quickly.

Market faces decisive test

The critical zone remains $157. A clean reclaim of this level, supported by rising spot inflows and controlled derivatives buildup, opens the path to $171 and then $175, where the 200-day EMA sits. That is the technical level that must be recovered to start rebuilding a durable uptrend.

If price fails at resistance, the structure shifts back toward lower support with risk of acceleration. The broader futures landscape supports this caution, with elevated leverage and unstable funding rates pointing to a market still prone to sharp reversals. Solana’s current setup reflects a market that has flushed weak hands but has yet to attract strong hands. Until spot inflows stabilize and trend resistance breaks, the asset remains in a corrective phase rather than a reversal cycle.

Previously, we discussed Solana’s vulnerability as long as the price remained below the descending trendline and major EMAs. The present structure reinforces those concerns, with flows, leverage, and resistance levels collectively limiting upside until momentum proves sustainable.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Wendy's: Too Cheap To Ignore While It Tries To Revitalize The Brand
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

The Wendy's Company (WEN) is undergoing a significant turnaround, and with the stock down about 50% over the past year due to several concerns, getting to a point where the stock seems oversold.

I’ll add that quality concerns around Wendy’s overall service are not enough to justify this year’s ~50% drop, and we’re instead looking at a mix of that combined with guidance cuts, constant US sales drops compared to stabilizing or even slightly improving competitors, significant dividend cut, the CEO departure in July (he went to Hershey (HSY) instead), uncertainty around Project Fresh, and also likely the core issue - losing the “value war” for budget-conscious consumers.

All of those combined with the premium pricing strategy in a struggling environment with weaker discretionary budgets can justify this loss in market confidence, but such a deep drop also leaves room for upside, as the company can improve significantly from this point.

Given the higher CAPEX, Wendy’s free cash flow reached about $195.6 million over the first 9 months of 2025, down from $213 million over 9M’24. As a note, they also changed their definition of free cash flow to properly show the expenditures related to their franchise development fund, which I would’ve counted even if they didn’t since it’s still CAPEX even if they include it separately, in my opinion, so it’s an overall improvement in transparency.

WEN cut its guidance in most regards to now reflect a 3% to 5% drop in global sales, while the free cash flow is now $35 million higher thanks to a cut in CAPEX, expecting Q4 to be flat or slightly up given the YTD results. At midpoint, we’re looking at $202.5 million, which is not bad at all for a $1.62 billion market cap - especially compared to their peers.

Financially, based on Wendy’s latest report, we see a relatively normal position for a company like this, with current assets slightly below their current liabilities and quite a lot of debt. As a note, the balance sheet is generally weak in the food serving industry, especially since Wendy’s has so many franchises since they are ultimately service providers, and often go for leasing instead of owning the real estate. This is the case for most companies in the industry, and I pointed it out for Starbucks (SBUX) in this recent analysis as well - whose equity is actually negative.

Another thing to keep in mind when looking into these financials is that the brand can’t be included unless it’s acquired, and even then, it’s bundled together in intangibles and goodwill, while in reality this brand value can change overnight (scandals, boycotts, etc.) and it’s ultimately a matter of how much a buyer would pay for it.

The brand is very important, especially since we’re talking about a business with a significant number of franchises, as growing popularity and international marketing can attract more partners going forward, and their recent actions seem to be meant to revitalize/improve it.

This debt is spread over several years, and aside from the 7% debentures due in December 2025 and the 3.78% notes due in 2026, there’s nothing coming until 2028. This can offer a breathing room for Wendy’s while they work on turning the business around, while further rate cuts can likely help them refinance at relatively low yields - similar to the current ones.

Dividends & Buybacks

As of the latest report, Wendy's remains on track to deliver over $325 million in shareholder returns, with $0.14 per share paid as dividends and 1.4 million shares repurchased during Q3. Annualized, that’s a dividend yield of about 6.6%, which is certainly not something to ignore since it costs about $107 million, making it very sustainable even for a weaker year. This comes after WEN cut its dividend earlier in 2025 from $0.25, which marked a very significant ~44% cut.

Still, I’d like to see them ramp up the buybacks today, but we have to understand that even though it saves them 6.6% (the cost of the dividend), this isn’t free. Even though it may be an excellent move to ramp up the buybacks after the stock fell over 50% in about a year, it may not be the best choice for Wendy’s given their turnaround efforts and capital optimization plans.

Opportunities

As part of their turnaround initiative, Wendy’s announced plans to close 200 to 350 of underperforming US restaurants through 2026, with their CFO and interim CEO highlighting:

We are working with our U.S. franchisees to evaluate each and every underperforming restaurant in our system from both a financial and a customer experience perspective and developing action plans for how to improve both. For some locations, it's about making operational changes or deploying technology. For others, we're improving productivity by aligning operating hours to better match demand, particularly in the morning and late-night dayparts.

This is part of Wendy’s “Project Fresh” strategy, aiming at replacing the weaker-performing restaurants with weak sales and poor customer satisfaction (a very important aspect) that cannot be fixed through simple operational changes or technology upgrades.

This could help address the concerns over Wendy’s overall quality of service/locations/etc., which has been a rising concern and even made the interim CEO emphasize that WEN can’t afford to carry this type of brand-damaging location anymore, aiming to re-focus on their “One Wendy's” alignments in an effort to revitalize the brand.

Something this company can still do is expand internationally, which is an advantage over peers like McDonald’s (MCD) or KFC (YUM) that have a much wider global presence. This can even fuel decades of growth going forward, and is a major contributor to Wendy’s recent surprise earnings beat, thanks to international markets (up 8.6% in terms of sales) offsetting the soft US market.

Rate cuts could also help WEN and especially the budget-conscious consumers significantly. In time, these cuts will be reflected in the company’s costs of debt (refinancing/issuing more), which is important given their significant US footprint. After all, international markets were what lifted Wendy’s results recently, while the US market continued to struggle and is not doing that well for virtually any other company that depends on the consumer’s discretionary budgets.

This can also encourage more investment activity from the company or potential franchisees and free up more money for marketing or other activities, which is ultimately a reason behind the Fed’s use of this tool. Again, this relative weakness is normal in a high-rate environment.

Meanwhile, FreshAI and advanced analytics are also getting to a significant size now, potentially allowing WEN to capitalize on consumer trends, with US digital sales up 14.9% YoY. Wendy’s generative AI voice ordering (FreshAI) is already being rolled out to 500 to 600 Wendy’s locations by the end of 2025, which is reportedly saving about 22 seconds off drive-thru ordering times, which is definitely very impressive if accurate.

To these, add the potential of a new CEO at least stabilizing the business on its path to a recovery and offering more certainty for the market, and we could see a recovery followed by potential for long-term growth going forward.

Risks To The Thesis

One risk specific to Wendy’s is that, at the same time, this lack of a definitive CEO, significant fall in valuation and rising uncertainty could also be taken advantage of by a peer or third party that could scoop them out at a lower price. Nelson Peltz’s Trian continues to have a significant stake of ~15%, although he stepped down after nearly 2 decades of leading as chair of Wendy’s board, potentially opening both a new era for the company, but also significant pressure in case they decide to sell like they did with Disney (DIS) in 2024.

This comes a few years after they made the stock jump 15% on the news that the fund was interested in a potential acquisition, while back in 2018, Papa John’s (PZZA) founder also held some merger talks with WEN. I say that this is both a risk and an opportunity because I could easily see a larger peer wanting to enjoy the turnaround, but that would also mean that nearly anyone that bought in recent history might get forced out at a loss.

Another potential issue to keep in mind can come from tariffs. As mentioned, we’re already talking about a company that’s fighting for a relatively weak discretionary budget, and continuing to raise prices because of higher tomato/other produce costs certainly wouldn’t help. It’s worth mentioning however that Wendy's has been de-risking its US beef supply chain and says that 100% of its beef comes from the US’ Beef Quality Assurance certified sources, so there’s at least a point of relief coming from that, but tariffs can lead to higher local costs as well.

We could also see some delays in rate cuts at least in the near term, with the US Government recently going through its longest shutdown in history elevating the market’s fears regarding these cuts, although that faded to some degree when some Fed officials said that they see the possibility of a December cut, but rates will remain elevated even after that.

Peer Comparison

Compared to a diverse set of peers including McDonald's, Burger King (QSR), YUM’s KFC/Taco Bell/etc., Shake Shack (SHAK) and Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG), we can see relative weakness in the industry, with the last 2 performing similarly to WEN over the past year. However, Wendy’s is miles away from an average or even low valuation compared to its peers. Right now, they are trading at 3 to 4 times the rest of the group’s P/CF ratio, and that’s even with relatively weak numbers combined with a 50% drop in stock price.

Valuation

Starting with $200 million in free cash flow (around Wendy’s updated guidance), let’s assume a CAGR of 8% over the first 5 years (below the EPS estimates available here, for comparison, and below their previous 2025 “long-term financial algorithm”) thanks to their turnaround initiatives, cost savings and potential international expansion helped by rate cuts, with a 5% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years and a 3.5% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 8% (above my estimated 6.4% based on a cost of equity of 10.6% and cost of debt of 4.7%), with $3.78 billion in net debt and 190.31 million shares outstanding based on the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $5.92 billion, an equity value of $2.14 billion and an intrinsic value of $11.22 per share.

Conclusions

I rate Wendy’s a buy thanks to their turnaround potential, still solid cash flows and long-term growth opportunities backed by their international expansion and macro tailwinds. Risks remain, of course, especially since they’re still searching for a permanent CEO during a significant turnaround initiative in such an environment, but the overall risk-reward is attractive after the stock fell so much.

data.title: The Prospects For The Leveraged JNUG ETF In 2026
data.description:
Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) did not wait long to establish its ninth consecutive quarterly gain and a new record high in Q4 2025. Nearby continuous COMEX gold futures reached a record high of $3,899.20 on September 30, 2025, and traded to a higher record high of $3,922.70 on October 1, before reaching $4,398 on October 20. While gold futures corrected to a low of $3,901.30 on October 28, they closed November at over $4,200 per ounce.

Gold’s bull market remains firmly intact in late 2025, and the prospect for even higher prices remains high as the market moves into 2026. The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF (JNUG) turbocharges junior gold mining share prices on the upside and could be a valuable trading tool over the coming months if gold’s ascent continues.

Gold’s bullish trend continues and enters its 27th year

At its most recent high of $4,398 per ounce, gold was 66.5% above its closing price at the end of 2024. Meanwhile, the leading precious metal has experienced explosive price appreciation since the turn of this century.

The 30-year monthly continuous contract chart highlights gold’s ascent from the 1999 low of $252.80 to the most recent high, which was over 17 times higher than the turn of the century bottom. As 2026 approaches, gold enters its 27th year of a bull market.

Meanwhile, the quarterly chart shows that gold prices have reached new record highs over the past nine consecutive quarters. A move over $4,398 per ounce in Q1 2026 will extend the streak to ten quarters.

Gold’s ascent reflects the descent of fiat currency values

Gold is the world’s oldest means of exchange. For thousands of years, and long before there were dollars, euros, pounds, yen, and other foreign exchange currencies, gold was a store of value and a form of money. The Bible’s Old Testament, the oldest sections of which date back to 1200 BCE, refers to gold. However, gold’s role could date back much further, with archaeological evidence suggesting that humans interacted with the metal as far back as 40,000 BCE.

In 1999, the Bank of England, the regulator for the international gold market, auctioned half its reserves, pushing the price to the $252.80 low, which is now fondly called the “Brown Bottom,” as Gordon Brown, the then Chancellor of the Exchequer, decided that gold as an asset was a barbarous relic. The U.K. sales marked a significant bottom in gold prices, which have since rallied. Over the past years, central banks have dramatically increased gold holdings.

While gold had backed some currencies during the previous century, today the legal tender issued by countries worldwide is fiat, deriving its value only from the full faith and credit of the countries and governments that issue currencies. Gold’s ascent reflects the steady decline in the value of fiat currencies. Central banks, governments, and monetary authorities validate gold’s role in the global financial system by holding it as a reserve asset, classifying it as a currency reserve. In 2025, gold replaced the euro as the second-leading reserve asset, behind only the U.S. dollar.

Lower U.S. interest rates favor higher gold

Gold is an interest rate-sensitive asset, like stocks and other assets. When real interest rates rise, the opportunity cost of holding gold as an asset increases, since it offers no yield. When rates decline, gold’s price tends to increase. However, rising interest rates due to inflation are another story, as gold has a long history as an inflation indicator. The bottom line is that inflation erodes fiat currencies’ purchasing power, while gold’s limited supply and scarcity maintain its value. While governments can issue currency to their heart’s content, the only way to increase the gold stock is to extract it from the earth’s crust.

Central banks continue to buy, and gold producers continue to profit

On October 23, 2025, the Visual Capitalist published a chart highlighting the growth in central bank gold purchases over the past decade.

JP Morgan forecasts that central banks will purchase another 900 metric tons to add to reserves in 2025. Meanwhile, the official data is likely lower than the actual government gold reserve purchases and holdings. According to the World Gold Council, China is the world’s leading gold producer, followed by Russia. China and Russia have been substantial gold buyers over the past years, and they have likely increased their reserves through domestic production. Since gold and other strategic commodity reserves are national security matters in China and Russia, the increase in their holdings is likely understated.

The bottom line is that gold holdings by country are rising, despite the parabolic price rally that has taken the gold price over 17 times higher since the low in 1999.

Gold-producing companies have seen their profits rise as the gold price has risen. Higher gold prices allow miners to process lower-grade ores, thereby increasing profits. Meanwhile, the gold bull market has caused investors and speculators to increase holdings in gold mining companies. Senior gold miners extract, produce, and process the metal. Junior gold miners explore the earth for gold reserves and produce in the early stages. Junior gold mining shares tend to be more speculative than those of senior miners because of their revenue flows. The senior miners have a steady income from production and processing, using hedging techniques to make their output economic if gold prices decline. Junior miners that locate new reserves can experience explosive price action in their shares. Therefore, the bull market in gold in 2025 has caused senior gold mining companies to outperform gold on a percentage basis, while junior gold mining companies have outperformed the seniors this year.

  • Gold rose 66.5% from $2,641 at the end of 2024 to the October 2025 high of $4,398 per ounce.
  • The GDX ETF, which owns shares of the leading senior gold mining companies, rose 150.9% from $33.91 at the end of 2024 to the October 2025 high of $85.08 per share.
  • The VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), which owns shares of the leading junior gold mining companies, rose 163% from $42.75 at the end of 2024 to the October 2025 high of $112.45 per share.

JNUG far outperformed gold futures in 2025 - Its leverage requires careful attention to risk-reward dynamics

Many analysts are calling for the gold bull to continue to charge higher in 2026. Goldman Sachs’ co-head of commodities forecasts that gold prices will reach $4,900 by the end of 2026. Meanwhile, other analysts and financial institutions expect prices to reach $5,000 per ounce or higher. If gold prices continue on their bullish path, senior and junior gold mining shares will likely continue to outperform the metal on a percentage basis.

The Direxion Daily Junior Gold Miners Index Bull 2X Shares ETF is a leveraged product that amplifies the price action in GDXJ. JNUG’s holdings include:

JNUG uses swaps and options to create leverage that turbocharges its performance relative to GDXJ on the upside. However, when GDXJ declines, JNUG will underperform the junior gold mining ETF by a wide margin.

At $177.03 per share, JNUG had $531.89 million in assets under management. JNUG trades an average of nearly 307,000 shares per day and charges a 1.02% management fee. While JNUG pays a $1.17 dividend, which translates to a 0.66% annual yield, its leverage makes it appropriate only for short-term long positions in junior gold mining shares.

From the end of 2024 to the October 2025 highs, gold rose 66.5%, GDX was 150.9% higher, and GDXJ rose 163%.

Over the same period, the leveraged JNUG ETF rose 468.5%, from $36.49 to $207.44 per share.

The potential for oversize gains comes with commensurate risks. The options and swaps that create JNUG’s leverage will destroy value when gold and gold mining shares decline or remain stable. 2025 was a unique year, as gold’s rally was parabolic. There are no guarantees that the parabolic trend will continue in 2026.

Leverage comes at a price, which is time decay. Since 2014, JNUG has experienced five value-destroying reverse splits.

The chart highlights JNUG’s share splits and reverse splits since 2014.

JNUG’s Seeking Alpha ETF Grades earn the highest possible A+ grade after the 2025 parabolic price action. The assets and daily trading volume receive an A- in the liquidity category. While JNUG earns a B in dividends, which do not matter for a short-term trading tool, the gearing results in failing grades in the expenses and risk categories.

When approaching leveraged junior gold mining exposure with JNUG, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to losses larger than those in junior gold mining shares. Moreover, stable junior gold mining share prices are likely to cause JNUG to decline, as time decay erodes its value. The swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risks. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as JNUG.

I remain bullish on gold and senior and junior gold mining shares as the market heads into 2026. While JNUG is a valuable trading tool, I would only buy with a clear risk-reward plan that includes price and time stops. JNUG is not appropriate for long-term risk positions. However, buying JNUG during downside corrections and accepting small losses in the quest for significant profits can be a successful trading strategy for the coming year if the gold bull continues to charge higher. Remember, even the most aggressive bull markets rarely move in straight lines. Corrections become more likely the higher gold and gold mining shares rise.

data.title: Broadcom: Entering A New Stage Of Growth (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Broadcom (AVGO) will be going into q4’25 in a position of strength following a robust earnings season for the major cloud services providers (CSPs). With growing interest by the major AI developers, Anthropic (ANTHRO) and OpenAI (OPENAI), Broadcom may realize a significant uplift in revenue in the coming quarters. As a result of the optimistic outlook, I am reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating for AVGO shares with a price target of $528/share at 29.86x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Broadcom is set to report q4’25 earnings on Dec. 11, 2025, after market close. Analysts have revised Broadcom EPS estimates up 27x and down 3x in the last 90 days, suggesting an optimistic outlook for the coming earnings release.

You can review my post-q3’25 earnings coverage of Broadcom here:

Broadcom: AI Infrastructure Spend Set To Drive Significant Growth

Investment Thesis

The q3’25 earnings season among the CSPs was extremely constructive for the AI chip space, particularly as each of the CSPs signaled a significant uplift in capital spend for the coming fiscal year. Growth in capital deployment isn’t an empty promise either; the increased capital outlay among CSPs is largely driven by the growing backlogs for cloud services, which now sit at an aggregate $1.21 trillion. As a result of the growing demand for cloud services, I am now forecasting an aggregate capital outlay of $454 billion for calendar year 2026, up from an estimated $368.5 billion in 2025.

One factor that will shift the attention towards chipmakers like Broadcom is the verbiage used by major CSPs like Microsoft (MSFT) in the quarter, suggesting that capital spending going forward will largely be tied to building out compute capacity or investing in GPUs, CPUs, XPUs, and networking & data center interconnect equipment.

Microsoft isn’t alone in the matter; peer CSPs like Alphabet (GOOG) will be investing in large-scale data centers in the coming years to support increasing investments made by model developers like Anthropic & OpenAI. Valued at roughly $50 billion, Alphabet will be supplying 1mm Ironwood TPUs to support a 1GW data center facility for Anthropic as part of a cloud hosting agreement. In addition to this, Alphabet may be set to scale Ironwood beyond previous TPU iterations, as the chips are designed to compete at a high level with Nvidia’s leading GPUs. As part of this, Alphabet is currently in talks with Meta Platforms (META) to deploy its TPUs as Meta invests in expanding its data center footprint by a significant degree. Accordingly, Meta has plans to invest $600 billion through 2028 to expand its data center footprint to support its Superintelligence Labs. If Meta seeks to leverage Alphabet’s TPUs in these developments, Broadcom may realize a substantial uplift in the coming years for its custom silicon.

Amazon (AMZN) has also signed a similar agreement for supplying 1mm chips for Anthropic as the AI developer expands its support across multiple infrastructure platforms. Amazon also signed a multi-year agreement with OpenAI to support tens of millions of GPUs in a deal valued at $38 billion.

In q4’25, Broadcom signed a deal directly with OpenAI to co-develop and supply 10GW of custom AI accelerators. The deal involves both custom silicon and networking solutions, all packaged in a server rack to be deployed for OpenAI. The deal is expected to commence in 2h26 and continue through the end of 2029. Using napkin math to value the deal based on Alphabet’s $50 billion deal for 1GW of capacity, Broadcom’s deal could be valued at upwards of $500 billion, though this will likely involve data center hosting, power, non-IT infrastructure like power distribution, and other factors, rather than purely infrastructure provided by Broadcom.

Advanced Micro Devices’ (AMD) deal for 6GW of compute capacity is expected to generate “tens of billions of dollars in revenue for AMD,” suggesting Broadcom’s portion of the deal may be more aligned with AMD’s OpenAI agreement. With the presumption that Broadcom may be in a position to charge a premium over AMD’s GPU supply agreement given that Broadcom’s chips will be purpose-built for OpenAI, Broadcom’s chip deal could provide more revenue on a per-gigawatt basis. Considering the additional networking infrastructure going into the server racks, Broadcom’s deal could be well north of $100 billion.

Financial Forecast

Looking ahead to eq4’25, I’m forecasting Broadcom to generate $17.7 billion in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.88/share. I believe the catalyst from the q4’25 earnings call will be forward guidance for eFY26, particularly as it relates to the growth trajectory among its custom silicon deals with the CSPs as well as its deal directly with OpenAI. I believe analysts will be critical of the economies of scale factor and how these deals may play both top-line growth and incremental expansion at the operating margin.

Risks Related to Broadcom

Bull Case

Broadcom is quickly becoming a central competitor to Nvidia as CSPs and AI developers seek to create more purpose-built infrastructure to facilitate AI application development. With the combination of custom silicon and networking infrastructure, Broadcom may be well positioned to realize substantial tailwinds as an integrated rack-scale solution.

Bear Case

Concerns are mounting relating to the profitability of AI applications, particularly as OpenAI seeks to accelerate development through substantial upfront investments for AI infrastructure. If investments were to slow down or if financing were to dry up, Broadcom may be left exposed to substantial risk of lost revenue.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AVGO shares are currently trading at 48.92x EV/aEBITDA, a relative premium when considering shares’ historical trading range. Given the significant uplift in revenue Broadcom may experience in the coming years, I’m expecting operations to catch up to the market capitalization, narrowing the premium over time. Considering the timing of the expected revenue uplift, AVGO shares will likely appear relatively expensive for the next 2-3 quarters.

Given the strong growth forecast, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for AVGO shares with a price target of $528/share at 29.86x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Kroger: This Time I Disagree With The Strategy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Two headwinds are hitting retailers: sticky inflation of 3% and cautious and value-seeking behaviors from lower-income cohorts. Last quarter, we saw that Kroger (KR) was able to cut prices and expand margins at the same time, thus making me state that the grocery retailer was delivering a masterclass to its peers, investing in price to win in volume. It may be easy to say, but it is hard to achieve. In fact, the company divested from its pharmacy business and leaned more toward private labels and focused on building a larger online scale. After Q2, we know that the company increased its guidance and expected FY sales to grow by at least 2.7% (ex-fuel). Traffic was improving, private label items gained market share, and the online channel was actually increasing sales, rather than partly cannibalizing them. In particular, we heard from Kroger's management that consumers visiting its stores were indeed trading down, but choosing premium private-label items, thus improving Kroger's profits.

This week we have talked about a two-speed economy where households with low incomes struggle and reduce their spending, while higher income earners, though reducing their savings rate, are still able to keep up with their style of living.

Kroger's earnings also help us understand better how consumers are behaving. As a matter of fact, when we see categories such as "Fresh" or "Our Brands" that outperform, we can infer that consumers are cooking more at home, thus hurting restaurants. When consumers are cost-conscious, Kroger usually experiences a favorable setup. But, as always, a favorable macroeconomic environment doesn't necessarily imply that a single retailer will do well: competition in this sector is high, and gaining even a tiny fraction of a percentage in market share means millions for companies of the scale of Kroger.

Kroger's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

With the setup we describe, the market was expecting Kroger to report a beat. Well, its results are mixed. On revenues, the company reported $33.86B, coming in below estimates by $320M. However, it managed to achieve an adj. EPS beat by $0.02 to $1.05. So, we are seeing in one day Dollar General move up by 10% while Kroger loses 7% at the time of writing.

Let's unpack the report to see where the issues occurred. Identical sales ex-fuel increased by 2.6%, while analysts expected 2.9%. This alone was enough to trigger a re-rating of the stock. Gross margin increased by 40 bps to 22.8% of sales, showing the positive effect of the divestiture from the specialty pharmacy business.

Kroger also informed us that it bought back shares aggressively under an accelerated program worth $5B, announcing that it will spend another $2.5B during Q4. Surely, the company's dry powder can now take advantage of the share price drop caused by the earnings report. However, the company's leverage ratio increased from 1.21 to 1.73 in one year. This is because, through 2024, the company issued more debt. Kroger targets a net total debt/EBITDA ratio of around 2.4x, which means the company will probably take on new debt to fund its share repurchases. The consequence is that we are seeing higher interest rate expenses that have almost doubled in one year. In Q3 Kroger paid $146M, while in Q3 of last year it paid $86M.

I understand that companies can leverage their balance sheet and use their financial structure to return capital to the shareholders. But, even though companies can choose to do so, I wonder why Kroger couldn't truly decide to invest this capital in lower prices to attract more traffic. True, the company's net income margin is thin (1.9%), but even adding a few basis points as an investment to attract more traffic could pay off. Kroger did indeed hint that its sales won't grow as fast as first promised because it will battle for price affordability, but just by not taking on new debt, it would have the millions to invest in this fight without increasing the risk of its balance sheet. I am not completely against debt, of course, but I think it is unwise when companies take on new debt mainly to fund share repurchases. True, share repurchases help the company's EPS, but paying less interest would too.

Now, Kroger updated its FY guidance, and investors didn't read what they expected. Instead of seeing it raised higher, Kroger is narrowing its ranges, tilting to the low end of the guidance, the most important metric: ID sales. While we were expecting the company to grow ID sales at 3.05% at the previous midpoint of the guidance, we now have a midpoint of 2.9%. This is news that investors aren't liking. Everything else is confirmed, but retailers are top-line companies. If volumes don't perform as people expect, then the stock will see its multiples compress.

Kroger's Valuation

After having more than doubled in five years, the stock is retracing back from its ATH of $75 reached this summer and now trades below $62. The fwd PE is 13.8, and the fwd EV/EBITDA is 7.9. These are low multiples, especially when we look at other retailers. Leaving aside Costco and Walmart, which trade at tech-like multiples, even discount stores such as Dollar General and Dollar Tree trade at an earnings multiple in the high-teens if not the low-twenties. Given Kroger's profitability and growth metrics, barely above 2%, these multiples make sense. On profitability, the company scores well because of its high return rates, which makes me wonder even more why the company is borrowing cash for buybacks instead of investing its organic cash in its business. However, its margins are thin and this clearly compresses the earnings multiple that investors are willing to give to the company.

All in all, I think this last quarterly report marks a setback for a company that was seeing positive momentum. Whether this will be just a one-time incident or the start of a new negative phase remains to be seen. Q4, in this regard, will prove much more important. It is usually the strongest quarter for grocery retailers, and it will allow us to see how Kroger is doing. A quarter ago, I considered Kroger a buy. In case the business had remained intact, I would have written that the post-earnings drop is an opportunity. But this time, I think the market rightly reflects a deterioration of the outlook and thus compresses the multiple. As a result, I am downgrading Kroger from buy to hold and suggest monitoring Kroger's Q4 earnings to better understand how things are unfolding.

data.title: Mixed Growth And Deflationary Pressures: 3 Calls For Asia
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava, Regional Head of Research, Asia-Pacific and Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

ING's base call: A weaker growth outlook for Asia

While recent trade agreements signal a de-escalation in tensions, they offer limited assurance that export growth will remain robust. The reduced tariff gap between China and its regional peers stands out as a clear strategic win for Beijing. However, most of ASEAN, except Singapore, now face 19-20% tariffs. The full impact of this is yet to be seen on export growth, which has been rather resilient in 2025. Softer exports and weaker growth are likely to remain headwinds for several regional currencies. Countries with strong exposure to AI-related exports may outperform, but for others, the agreements do little to counteract the broader slowdown in trade momentum.

We expect GDP growth in Korea and Japan to accelerate in 2026 as substantial fiscal stimulus filters through to household consumption. In contrast, downside risks have intensified for domestic demand-driven economies such as India and Indonesia in the near term, which should leave overall growth in Asia weaker compared to 2025. India’s inability to secure a trade agreement with the US remains a key concern unless negotiations progress. Meanwhile, Indonesia’s growth outlook is vulnerable to investment headwinds stemming not only from tariff-related uncertainty but also from subdued government spending.

Our risky call: Deflationary pressures persist across the continent

While unfavourable base effects may cause inflation readings to edge higher next year, we expect the key drivers behind the slowdown this year to persist.

First, consumer spending across Asia remains subdued despite easing inflation, as labour markets continue to show weakness. Discretionary spending is likely to stay constrained, reflecting a K-shaped post-Covid recovery that has deepened income disparities at the lower end. This dynamic points to continued softness in core inflation, as weak demand limits businesses’ pricing power.

Second, fuel prices are likely to remain benign, supported by stable supply conditions and moderate global demand. This will further alleviate cost pressures across transportation and manufacturing sectors.

Third, persistent overcapacity in China is widening the demand-supply gap in Asia, exerting downward pressure on prices. This deflationary trend is likely to continue spilling over into regional markets.

Our bold call: Government incentives could boost the Korean won

Korea's economic growth is projected to accelerate to 2.0% YoY in 2026 (vs 1.2% in 2025), supported by government spending and steady export performance. Continued strong global demand for AI chips is expected, while the weak KRW is expected to help offset some of the adverse effects of the 15% US tariffs. Should demand for semiconductors exceed expectations, GDP growth in 2026 could grow even further to 2.3% YoY.

With inflation close to 2%, a faster closure of the negative output gap, and little property price stabilisation, the Bank of Korea may end its easing sooner than expected, possibly leading to stronger-than-expected KRW appreciation.

The government also intends to increase incentives for equity market investment, enhance shareholder rights, and improve corporate governance to support Korea's goal of attaining developed-market status. These initiatives, combined with solid large-cap performance (primarily from leading Korean exporters) and enhanced accessibility to the Korean FX market, could attract further capital inflows into Korean asset markets while tempering outbound investment activity by domestic investors.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: What The 50-Year Mortgage Debate Reveals About U.S. Housing Constraints
data.description:
By Julia R. Cartwright

The US housing market in late 2025 is defined by contradictory forces: rising prices but slowing growth, increasing inventory but falling affordability, and a demographic shift that is weakening long-run demand even as short-run supply remains structurally constrained.

Against this backdrop, President Trump’s proposal for a 50-year mortgage is an attempt to stretch affordability in a market that has outpaced incomes, and it exposes deeper issues. Mortgage duration is both a financial feature and a policy artifact shaped by decades of government intervention dating back to the New Deal. A 50-year mortgage may expand access by lowering monthly payments, but it also dramatically increases lifetime interest costs and could raise prices depending on supply elasticity. The debate over this proposal is ultimately a debate over the real frictions in the housing market, namely interest-rate lock-in, constrained supply, and the institutional architecture that prevents solutions like portable mortgages from being widely available.

The American housing market rarely changes in sudden leaps. Prices adjust gradually, construction responds slowly, and mortgage product design barely shifts at all. That is why the mere suggestion of a 50-year mortgage by President Trump is so economically revealing. If housing finance policymakers are floating half-century debt structures, something fundamental in the market is out of balance.

Today’s housing market presents a strange tableau: home prices are still rising, but at a slowing pace. According to the latest Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) data, prices are up roughly 2.2 percent year-over-year in Q3 2025. Sales have ticked up modestly, with existing home transactions rising 1.2 percent in October. Inventory is finally improving, up about 12.6 percent year over year, driven mostly by new construction rather than existing homes. Mortgage rates have eased from their peaks and now sit in the six-percent range for many buyers. On paper, this resembles a soft landing, but in reality, the market remains defined by broad affordability stress.

Many homes are sitting unsold for long stretches, not because buyers are absent but because sellers are holding out for pandemic-era prices. Renters’ expectations of becoming homeowners have collapsed from 52.6 percent in 2019 to just 33.9 percent today, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. And demographic headwinds are emerging: births are declining, population growth is slowing, and long-run demand will weaken as the nation ages. Housing should be cooling naturally, yet it isn’t. The affordability crisis is so acute in the short run that policymakers are reaching for financial engineering solutions rather than addressing structural constraints.

It is amidst this backdrop that Trump proposed the 50-year mortgage, casting himself in the lineage of major housing finance reforms, much like President Roosevelt’s role in ushering in the modern 30-year mortgage during the New Deal. By extending loan terms, the administration argues it can meaningfully lower monthly payments and open the door to homeownership for buyers priced out of today’s market. In that narrow sense, the idea appears palatable; when affordability is collapsing and buyers are increasingly constrained by monthly cash flow, stretching the mortgage horizon looks like an intuitive policy lever. But as with any major change in mortgage design, the economic logic is more complicated.

A longer mortgage lowers monthly payments at the cost of paying much more interest over many more years. For a median-priced $415,000 home purchased with an FHA loan at 3.5 percent down, here is how the math works:

Mortgage Term Interest Rate Monthly Payment Number of Payments Total of All Payments Total Interest Paid
15-year 5.5% $3,272.21 180 $588,998.69 $188,523.69
30-year 5.99% $2,398.48 360 $863,451.30 $462,976.30
50-year 6.4% $2,227.44 600 $1,336,462.35 $935,987.35

The 50-year mortgage trims the monthly payment relative to the 30-year, but at the cost of doubling the total interest burden. For households focused solely on monthly cash flow, particularly first-time buyers, this tradeoff can appear worth it. A lower payment either gets a family into the home they want or allows them to buy a more expensive house. Economically, it functions like any intertemporal tradeoff: more affordability now, much higher cost later.

Critics argue that introducing ultra-long mortgages will push home prices higher. The answer is that it depends entirely on supply elasticity. In markets where new housing is constrained by zoning, permitting delays, or not-in-my-backyard (NIMBY)-driven land use restrictions, extended mortgage terms can, in fact, capitalize into higher prices. In elastic markets, the effect is muted. This is not a moral failing of the policy; it is simple microeconomics. If your policy goal is to increase homeownership, you accept certain tradeoffs, just as every country with 40- to 100-year mortgages has.

What often gets lost in the discussion is that the United States did not adopt the 30-year mortgage because markets naturally arrived at it. The product is fundamentally the outcome of government intervention. During FDR’s New Deal, the Federal Housing Administration standardized long-term amortized mortgages, displacing the short-term, interest-only loans that had dominated before the Great Depression. Fannie Mae later expanded liquidity and uniformity in mortgage finance. The 30-year mortgage was authorized by Congress in the late 1940s and eventually became dominant because federal agencies guaranteed it.

In other words, the “normal” American mortgage is not a market creation; it is a political one. A 50-year mortgage would simply be the next step in an 80-year continuum of policy-driven mortgage evolution.

The deeper issue in the housing market is not the absence of exotic mortgage products. It is that existing homeowners are frozen in place. Millions of households locked in 2-4 percent mortgage rates during the pandemic. With current rates near six percent, these homeowners don’t want to move, even when downsizing or scaling up might make sense. That keeps existing inventory off the market. Meanwhile, a record share of homes for sale are new construction, not existing properties.

One innovative solution would be portable mortgages, where the borrower could keep their existing mortgage rate but shift the collateral to a new home. Instead of being trapped in their current house because of a pandemic-era 3 percent mortgage, a household could sell, buy a different property, and simply move the lien from House A to House B. In theory, this would dramatically improve mobility, unfreeze existing home inventory, and loosen one of the tightest bottlenecks in the current housing market: interest rate lock-in.

But portable mortgages do not exist in the United States for reasons deeply rooted in the structure of American mortgage finance. The US system is built around long-term, fixed-rate mortgages that are pooled into mortgage-backed securities, financial instruments priced according to the specific borrower and the specific property at the moment the loan is issued. Letting borrowers carry their old loan to a new house would upend that securitization model, causing investors to absorb unknown collateral risk midstream and making the securities far harder to price.

The dominance of the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage compounds the issue. If borrowers could port low rates across multiple moves, they would have little reason to refinance, starving lenders of the fee income and interest rate resets they depend on to originate new loans. Investors could also face greater duration risk, being stuck earning three percent for decades even as market rates rise, causing them to demand higher rates across the entire mortgage market. And unlike countries such as the UK or Canada, where portable mortgages are common, US mortgages are secured by a specific property for the life of the loan and fixed for thirty years, not two to five.

Changing collateral midstream would require new appraisals, new legal filings, and a fundamental reengineering of mortgage securitization. All of this means that while portable mortgages could meaningfully improve housing mobility, they run directly counter to the incentives and infrastructure of the US mortgage system. Banks and investors prefer refinancing into higher rates, and the legal plumbing is built around property-specific collateral, not borrower-specific contracts. As a result, portable mortgages remain economically appealing in theory but institutionally implausible in practice.

If, however, banks and regulators could find a way to make portability compatible with the existing system, it could be a genuine game changer for American homeowners. Imagine a world where a young couple who locked in a low rate on their starter home is not punished financially for having a third child and needing more space, or where empty nesters can downsize without watching their mortgage payment jump simply because they move. Portability would make the mortgage contract follow the household’s life cycle rather than anchor it to a single property, smoothing mobility across labor markets, helping people move closer to better jobs, and reducing the mismatch between housing stock and household needs.

This would mean more efficient use of the existing housing stock, less pressure to overbuild in certain markets, and a healthier, more dynamic relationship between housing and labor-market mobility. It is precisely because the gains to households and to the broader economy are so large that portable mortgages are worth serious experimentation, even if the institutional and regulatory hurdles are high.

Ultimately, the debate over 50-year mortgages is less about exotic loan structures and more about the deeper structural limits of America’s housing system. Affordability has deteriorated because supply is constrained, mobility is frozen, and our mortgage architecture has not evolved with economic realities.

Extending mortgage terms may offer short-term relief, but the real innovations, like portable mortgages or reforms that unlock supply, require rethinking the institutional plumbing that has defined US housing finance since the New Deal. If policymakers want lasting affordability rather than financial patches, they must address the structural forces that make such extreme proposals politically viable in the first place.
